Therizinosaurus: Cretaceous theropod with absurdly long scythe-like claws reaching nearly one meter in length.
Yi qi: Tiny Jurassic theropod with bat-like membranous wings stretched between elongated fingers.
Deinocheirus: Massive ornithomimid with a sail-backed hump, broad duck-bill, and enormous hooked claws.
Amargasaurus: Sauropod with parallel rows of tall neural spines forming a double sail along its neck.
Nigersaurus: Broad-mouthed sauropod with over 500 replacement teeth arranged in battery-like rows for vacuum feeding.
Kosmoceratops: Ceratopsian sporting fifteen horns and spikes, the most ornamented face of any dinosaur discovered.
Concavenator: Carcharodontosaur with a prominent triangular hump before its hips and possible quill knobs.
Helicoprion: Paleozoic shark with a bizarre spiral whorl of teeth forming a circular saw in its lower jaw.
Dunkleosteus: Armored placoderm fish with guillotine-like bone plates instead of teeth, apex predator of Devonian seas.
Quetzalcoatlus: Giraffe-sized azhdarchid pterosaur with ten-meter wingspan, stalking prey on foot like a stork.
Kaprosuchus: Crocodilian with forward-facing tusks and upright limbs, nicknamed 'boar croc' from the Sahara.
Tanystropheus: Bizarre Triassic reptile with a neck longer than its body and tail combined.
Styracosaurus: Ceratopsian with six long horns radiating from its neck frill like a spiky crown.
Nothronychus: Pot-bellied therizinosaur with long claws, feathers, and a herbivorous diet despite theropod ancestry.
Sinosauropteryx: First dinosaur confirmed with fossilized feathers, displaying ginger and white striped tail pattern.
Hatzegopteryx: Giant azhdarchid pterosaur with robust skull built for killing prey, apex predator of Cretaceous Romania.
Dimetrodon: Sail-backed synapsid predator from the Permian, not a dinosaur but often mistaken for one.
Shonisaurus: Bus-sized ichthyosaur reaching fifteen meters, the largest marine reptile of the Triassic period.
Citipati: Crested oviraptor with cassowary-like head crest, famously fossilized while brooding its nest.
Dracorex: Dome-headed pachycephalosaur with spiky horns and dragon-like appearance, possibly juvenile Pachycephalosaurus.
Tyrannosaurus rex: Apex predator with bone-crushing jaws, tiny arms, and binocular vision for hunting.
Pachyrhinosaurus: Ceratopsian with massive bony boss replacing the nose horn, living in Arctic regions.
Majungasaurus: Stocky abelisaurid with stubby horn and evidence of cannibalism among its own species.
Archelon: Giant sea turtle reaching four meters, the largest turtle ever discovered from Cretaceous seas.
Gorgonops: Saber-toothed therapsid predator from the Permian with massive canines and mammal-like features.
Dimorphodon: Puffin-faced pterosaur with large head, short neck, and two distinct tooth types.
Saltasaurus: Armored titanosaur sauropod with osteoderms embedded in its skin for protection.
Suchomimus: Crocodile-snouted spinosaurid with elongated skull and thumb claws for catching fish.
Corythosaurus: Duck-billed hadrosaur with tall semicircular head crest used for vocal resonance chambers.
Sarcosuchus: Forty-foot 'super croc' from Cretaceous Africa, large enough to prey on dinosaurs.
Nyctosaurus: Pteranodontid with enormous antler-like head crest extending twice its skull length.
Magyarosaurus: Dwarf sauropod from island habitats, only six meters long due to insular dwarfism.
Baryonyx: Spinosaurid with crocodilian jaws, massive thumb claws, and fish scales in fossilized stomach.
Kronosaurus: Short-necked pliosaur with massive skull and banana-sized teeth for crushing marine prey.
Sinoceratops: Only confirmed ceratopsian from China, with unique forward-curving frill horns and boss.
Postosuchus: Rauisuchian archosaur standing upright on four legs, top predator before dinosaurs dominated.
Tupuxuara: Tapejarid pterosaur with tall semicircular head crest and toothless beak for frugivorous diet.
Ouranosaurus: Iguanodontid with tall neural spines forming a sail or hump along its back.
Scutosaurus: Heavily armored pareiasaur from the Permian with bony knobs covering its entire body.
Thalassomedon: Elasmosaur with exceptionally long neck containing seventy-two vertebrae for ambush feeding.
Ceratosaurus: Theropod with prominent nose horn and row of osteoderms running down its spine.
Estemmenosuchus: Therapsid with bizarre antler-like bony protrusions growing from its cheeks and forehead.
Edmontosaurus: Hadrosaurid with mummified specimens preserving skin, crest, and evidence of stripes.
Sharovipteryx: Gliding archosaur with wing membranes stretched between elongated hind legs, not forelimbs.
Jeholopterus: Anurognathid pterosaur with enormous eyes, wide frog-like mouth, and fuzzy pycnofibers coating.
Giganotosaurus: Carcharodontosaur slightly larger than T. rex with blade-like teeth for slicing flesh.
Triceratops: Three-horned ceratopsian with massive frill, the iconic tank of the Late Cretaceous.
Sinornithosaurus: Small feathered dromaeosaur possibly possessing venomous grooved teeth for subduing prey.
Basilosaurus: Serpentine early whale reaching eighteen meters, with vestigial hind legs and carnivorous diet.
Stegosaurus: Plated dinosaur with double row of kite-shaped back plates and spiked thagomizer tail.
Carnotaurus: Horned abelisaurid with exceptionally short snout, vestigial arms, and distinctive bull-like brow horns protruding above eyes.
Microraptor: Four-winged dromaeosaur with iridescent black feathers, gliding between trees using leg and arm feathers.
Parasaurolophus: Duck-billed hadrosaur with elaborate curved head crest extending backward over six feet for vocalization.
Spinosaurus: Sail-backed semiaquatic predator with crocodilian jaws, paddle tail, and dense bones for underwater hunting.
Epidexipteryx: Bizarre scansoriopterygid with four elongated ribbon-like tail feathers and buck-toothed protruding front teeth.
Psittacosaurus: Beaked ceratopsian ancestor with porcupine-like bristles sprouting from tail, displaying countershading coloration patterns.
Mamenchisaurus: Long-necked sauropod with neck comprising half total body length, reaching nineteen vertebrae.
Yutyrannus: House-sized tyrannosauroid covered in shaggy filamentous feathers despite its massive three-ton weight.
Pachycephalosaurus: Dome-headed dinosaur with nine-inch-thick skull roof surrounded by bony spikes and knobs.
Deinosuchus: Giant alligator relative reaching twelve meters, powerful enough to prey upon large dinosaurs.
Kulindadromeus: Ornithischian with filamentous feather-like structures, proving fuzzy integument evolved independently multiple times.
Limusaurus: Toothless ceratosaur losing teeth with age, transitioning from omnivorous juvenile to herbivorous adult.
Scelidosaurus: Primitive armored dinosaur covered in rows of keeled osteoderms forming early body armor.
Masiakasaurus: Small noasaurid with forward-projecting front teeth designed for snatching fish and small prey.
Stygimoloch: Spiky-headed pachycephalosaur with horns and nodes, possibly juvenile growth stage of Pachycephalosaurus.
Argentinosaurus: Titanosaur reaching thirty-five meters and seventy tons, among the largest land animals ever.
Eoraptor: Crow-sized early dinosaur from Late Triassic, displaying transitional features between major dinosaur lineages.
Irritator: Spinosaurid named for frustrating preparation, with elongated crocodilian snout and sagittal head crest.
Cryolophosaurus: Antarctic theropod sporting distinctive pompadour-like head crest arching forward over skull.
Stomatosuchus: Enormous crocodilian with thin flat mandible like pelican pouch for filter-feeding in Cretaceous rivers.
Protoceratops: Sheep-sized early ceratopsian with prominent neck frill but no horns, abundant Gobi Desert fossil.
Anchiornis: Four-winged troodontid with reconstructed red-and-black-and-white plumage pattern from fossilized melanosomes.
Elasmosaurus: Extreme long-necked plesiosaur with tiny head, initially reconstructed with head on tail end.
Caudipteryx: Peacock-sized oviraptorosaur with symmetrical display feathers fanning from shortened tail.
Nothosaurus: Triassic semi-aquatic reptile with webbed feet, long neck, and needle-teeth for catching fish.
Sauropelta: Nodosaur covered in armor plating with enormous shoulder spikes protecting neck from theropod attacks.
Thanatosdrakon: Giant azhdarchid pterosaur from Argentina with nine-meter wingspan, contemporary with ground-stalking relatives.
Incisivosaurus: Buckteeth oviraptorosaur with enlarged rodent-like front incisors and herbivorous beak.
Repenomamus: Badger-sized Cretaceous mammal with juvenile Psittacosaurus remains preserved in stomach contents.
Temnodontosaurus: Dolphin-like ichthyosaur with enormous eyes thirty centimeters across for deep-water hunting.
Atopodentatus: Bizarre Triassic marine reptile with zipper-toothed hammer-shaped skull for filter-feeding algae.
Brachiosaurus: High-shouldered sauropod with giraffe-like forelimbs raising head thirteen meters above ground.
Anurognathus: Tiny fuzzy pterosaur with enormous eyes and wide mouth, hawking insects like aerial nightjar.
Pteranodon: Toothless pterosaur with backward-pointing head crest and seven-meter wingspan soaring over Cretaceous seas.
Eustreptospondylus: Medium-sized megalosaur known from single nearly complete juvenile skeleton from Jurassic England.
Megarachne: Giant Carboniferous eurypterid initially mistaken for spider, reaching sixty centimeters with paddle tail.
Bajadasaurus: Dicraeosaurid sauropod with forward-pointing neck spikes forming defensive mane along cervical vertebrae.
Liopleurodon: Short-necked pliosaur with massive skull, powerful flippers, and exaggerated pop-culture size reputation.
Eudimorphodon: Early pterosaur with multi-cusped teeth indicating fish diet, preserved with fish scales inside.
Borealopelta: Exceptionally preserved nodosaur with three-dimensional armor, stomach contents, and reddish countershaded coloration.
Tianyulong: Heterodontosaurid with hollow filamentous structures along back and tail, controversial protofeather evidence.
Mosasaurus: Massive marine lizard with double-hinged jaw, paddle limbs, and shark-like tail dominating Cretaceous oceans.
Velociraptor: Turkey-sized dromaeosaur with sickle claw, feathered body, and intelligence exaggerated by popular media.
Asilisaurus: Silesaurid dinosauriform with beak-like front teeth, showing transitional features preceding true dinosaurs.
Isisaurus: Stocky titanosaur with proportionally shorter neck and vertically-oriented neural spines forming low ridge.
Ctenochasma: Pterosaur with comb of hundreds of fine bristle-teeth for filter-feeding tiny crustaceans from water.
Deinotherium: Elephant relative with downward-curving lower tusks and no upper tusks, browsing Miocene vegetation.
Minmi: Fast-running ankylosaur with paravertebral armor plates fused to vertebrae, unique among armored dinosaurs.
Coelophysis: Slender Triassic theropod found in mass bone beds, possibly cannibalistic during drought conditions.
Regaliceratops: Ceratopsian with crown-like frill, short brow horns, and large nose horn inverting typical Triceratops pattern.
Beipiaosaurus: Large therizinosaur covered in downy feathers and bizarre elongated broad-feathered plumes along arms.
Diabloceratops: Ceratopsian with two large curved horns projecting sideways from frill, resembling devil horns.
Koolasuchus: Giant temnospondyl amphibian surviving into Cretaceous, four meters long with flattened crocodile-like skull.
Oxalaia: Brazilian spinosaurid known from fragmentary skull material, largest theropod discovered in South America.
Qianzhousaurus: Long-snouted tyrannosaur nicknamed 'Pinocchio rex' with elongated narrow skull unlike robust relatives.
Attenborosaurus: Small plesiosaur with relatively short neck and robust skull, named for David Attenborough.
Dracoraptor: Welsh theropod from earliest Jurassic with gracile build and sharp serrated teeth for hunting.
Guanlong: Crested early tyrannosauroid with prominent cassowary-like head crest, smaller than later relatives.
Pegomastax: Tiny heterodontosaurid with porcupine quills and vampire-like canine teeth despite herbivorous diet.
Rajasaurus: Indian abelisaurid with single rounded horn on forehead and ridged skull ornamentation.
Austroraptor: Large dromaeosaur with proportionally short arms and conical teeth for catching aquatic prey.
Epidendrosaurus: Tiny arboreal scansoriopterygid with elongated third finger for extracting insects from tree bark.
Orthoceras: Straight-shelled nautiloid from Ordovician seas reaching six meters, swimming vertically through water column.
Kunbarrasaurus: Australian ankylosaur with nasal passages forming complex coiled structures for thermoregulation or vocalization.
Macroplata: Early Jurassic plesiosaur with moderately long neck and limbs transitioning toward marine specialization.
Rugops: Wrinkle-faced abelisaurid with skull covered in vascular grooves, possibly supporting keratinous facial ornamentation.
Bagaceratops: Small Mongolian ceratopsian with modest frill and no prominent horns, sheep-sized desert dweller.
Dsungaripterus: Pterosaur with upward-curved jaw tips and flattened rear teeth for crushing shellfish.
Futalognkosaurus: Massive titanosaur known from unusually complete skeleton including articulated neck vertebrae.
Leptoceratops: Primitive small ceratopsian lacking horns, retaining bipedal agility unlike later quadrupedal relatives.
Metriorhynchus: Fully marine crocodilian with paddle limbs, shark-like tail fin, and salt-excreting glands.
Achillobator: Large dromaeosaur with robust build and enlarged sickle claw, Mongolian velociraptor relative.
Aerosteon: Allosauroid theropod with pneumatic bones showing air-sac system resembling modern bird respiratory anatomy.
Eotriceratops: Enormous ceratopsian with three-meter skull, among largest horned dinosaurs from latest Cretaceous.
Gastornis: Flightless terror bird with massive crushing beak, apex predator of Paleocene forests.
Ichthyovenator: Spinosaurid with distinctive double-sailed back formed by two separate peaks of neural spines.
Jingshanosaurus: Bulky prosauropod with robust limbs and spoon-shaped teeth for processing tough plant material.
Kryptops: Small abelisaurid with rugose face bones and forward-facing horn cores above eyes.
Mymoorapelta: Earliest known North American ankylosaur with armor plating and small shoulder spikes.
Nemicolopterus: Sparrow-sized pterosaur with curved claws suggesting arboreal climbing lifestyle in forest canopy.
Peloneustes: Compact pliosaur with proportionally large skull and banana-shaped teeth for gripping slippery prey.
Sciurumimus: Juvenile megalosaur preserved with feather impressions covering entire body including tail.
Udanoceratops: Large protoceratopsid lacking horns and possessing tall skull with powerful jaw muscles.
Variraptor: French dromaeosaur known from fragmentary remains showing typical sickle-clawed raptor characteristics.
Xenoceratops: Canadian ceratopsian with unique rectangular frill adorned with hook-like spikes pointing forward.
Zhenyuanlong: Short-armed dromaeosaur with large wings featuring pennaceous feathers despite flightless condition.
Borogovia: Tiny troodontid named after Lewis Carroll creature, known primarily from distinctive hindlimb bones.
Diamantinasaurus: Stocky Australian titanosaur with robust limb bones adapted for supporting massive weight.
Fruitadens: Smallest ornithischian dinosaur at seventy centimeters, heterodontosaurid with varied tooth types.
Harpactognathus: Jurassic pterosaur with robust skull and interlocking teeth for catching fish.
Juravenator: Compsognathid theropod showing both scales and simple filamentous feathers in different body regions.
Lingwulong: Earliest diplodocoid sauropod pushing family origins back fifteen million years into Middle Jurassic.
Pampadromaeus: Early sauropodomorph showing transitional features between basal dinosaurs and later giant sauropods.
Tanycolagreus: Slender coelurosaur with elongated legs built for pursuit predation in Morrison Formation ecosystems.
Unaysaurus: Brazilian early sauropodomorph with leaf-shaped teeth and grasping hands for omnivorous feeding.
Wuerhosaurus: Late-surviving stegosaur from Early Cretaceous China with lower, rounder back plates than relatives.
Zapalasaurus: Diplodocoid sauropod retaining primitive features like vertically-oriented neural spines on vertebrae.
Carcharodontosaurus: Massive African predator with shark-toothed jaws and blade-like teeth for slashing prey.
Dracovenator: Early dilophosaurid theropod with dual head crests and gracile build from South African Jurassic.
Thalassodromeus: Huge-crested tapejarid pterosaur with cranial crest comprising seventy-five percent of skull length.
Effigia: Toothless beaked crocodile relative from Triassic running on two legs, convergently resembling ornithomimid dinosaurs.
Erlikosaurus: Therizinosaur with deep snout and beak for herbivory, displaying transitional features toward plant-eating.
Hallucigenia: Cambrian worm with spines on back and tentacles below, initially reconstructed completely upside-down.
Ichthyostega: Four-limbed tetrapod transitioning from water to land, retaining fish-like tail and seven-toed feet.
Lythronax: Early tyrannosaur with wide skull and binocular vision, predating T. rex by ten million years.
Nanuqsaurus: Pygmy Arctic tyrannosaur adapted to polar winters, thirty percent smaller than southern relatives.
Opabinia: Cambrian arthropod with five eyes and vacuum-hose proboscis tipped with grasping claws.
Pterodaustro: Filter-feeding pterosaur with thousand bristle-like teeth forming flamingo-style strainer in lower jaw.
Rhamphocephalus: Long-necked pterosaur with needle teeth and pointed beak for spearing fish from water surface.
Tiktaalik: Fish-amphibian intermediate with wrist bones, neck, and lungs, crawling in Devonian streams.
Vulcanodon: Primitive sauropod showing transitional anatomy between prosauropods and giant long-necked forms.
Anomalocaris: Cambrian apex predator reaching one meter with compound eyes and circular toothed grasping appendages.
Dreadnoughtus: Colossal titanosaur weighing sixty tons with remarkably complete skeleton including tail and limbs.
Fukuiraptor: Japanese megaraptoran with enlarged hand claws and gracile build for active predation.
Gobivenator: Feathered troodontid with large eyes and grasping hands for nocturnal hunting in Mongolian desert.
Hylaeosaurus: Early armored dinosaur with shoulder spines and rows of bony plates protecting flanks.
Mapusaurus: Pack-hunting carcharodontosaur slightly smaller than Giganotosaurus, found in multi-individual bone bed.
Nanshiungosaurus: Slender therizinosaur with proportionally short claws and long neck for high browsing.
Ophthalmosaurus: Streamlined ichthyosaur with enormous eyes twenty centimeters across for twilight zone diving.
Pantydraco: Early prosauropod from Welsh fissure fills showing beginning of sauropodomorph radiation.
Sauroniops: Carcharodontosaur known from distinctive eye-ridged skull roof, nicknamed 'Eye of Sauron' predator.
Unenlagia: Large dromaeosaur with bird-like shoulder allowing wing-flapping motion despite flightless size.
Xiphactinus: Six-meter bulldog fish with fangs, often preserved with entire fish swallowed whole inside.
Barosaurus: Diplodocid with extremely long neck possibly rearing on hind legs to browse fifteen meters high.
Chilesaurus: Bizarre theropod convergently resembling ornithischians, with leaf-shaped teeth and two-toed feet.
Ekrixinatosaurus: Stocky abelisaurid from Argentina with deep skull and reduced arms typical of family.
Falcarius: Early therizinosaur transitioning toward herbivory with leaf-shaped teeth and pot-bellied body.
Glacialisaurus: Antarctic sauropodomorph showing massive dinosaurs inhabited polar regions during warmer Jurassic climate.
Huaxiagnathus: Large compsognathid with proportionally long arms and grasping hands for seizing prey.
Jinzhousaurus: Iguanodontid with prominent thumb spike and dental batteries for processing vegetation efficiently.
Kryptodrakon: Earliest pterodactyloid pterosaur bridging gap between long-tailed and short-tailed flying reptiles.
Lurdusaurus: Massively built iguanodontid with graviportal limbs adapted for wading in aquatic environments.
Neovenator: Large allosauroid from Isle of Wight with pneumatic skull bones and blade-like teeth.
Protarchaeopteryx: Flightless oviraptorosaur with symmetrical tail feathers and downy body covering.
Qianosuchus: Triassic archosaur with armor plating and upright gait predating true crocodilians.
Scleromochlus: Tiny Triassic ornithodiran with long hind legs built for hopping, possible pterosaur ancestor.
Teleocrater: Early bird-line archosaur with crocodile-like ankle, rewriting dinosaur ancestry understanding.
Ultrasauros: Enormous brachiosaurid sauropod reaching thirty meters, originally confused with Supersaurus material.
Wannanosaurus: Primitive dome-headed pachycephalosaur with flat thickened skull roof and small body size.
Yinlong: Earliest ceratopsian from Late Jurassic showing primitive features like bipedal stance.
Zuniceratops: Earliest ceratopsian with brow horns, bridging primitive and advanced horned dinosaur evolution.
Austroposeidon: Brazilian titanosaur known from tall neural spines indicating possible sail or hump.
Camptosaurus: Robust iguanodontid lacking thumb spike with powerful hind legs for bipedal locomotion.
Datousaurus: Primitive sauropod with spatulate teeth and stocky build from Middle Jurassic China.
Europasaurus: Dwarf macronarian sauropod from island habitat reaching only six meters at maturity.
Fukuisaurus: Japanese iguanodontid with complex dental batteries and prominent nasal crest structure.
Herrerasaurus: Early bipedal predator from Late Triassic showing primitive dinosaurian hip and skull features.
Istiodactylus: Broad-beaked pterosaur with spatula-shaped jaws for probing mud and filtering invertebrates.
Jobaria: Primitive macronarian sauropod retaining ancestral features like spoon-shaped teeth and straight neck.
Stokesosaurus: Small early tyrannosaur with blade-like teeth predating evolution of bone-crushing jaws.
